does: SafeSteer performs localized on-policy distillation to align LLMs with safety constraints while preserving general capabilities. The method constructs a safety teacher through activation steering, then identifies safety-relevant tokens where modifications are needed. During training, it applies reverse KL divergence penalty exclusively to these safety tokens rather than globally across the model, enabling targeted alignment. Experiments across multiple models demonstrate that SafeSteer achieves strong performance on seven safety benchmarks with minimal degradation on five general capability benchmarks, outperforming existing dual-objective balancing approaches that either require massive general-purpose datasets or auxiliary reward models.
connects: This work builds on the longstanding problem of alignment tax in LLM safety training, where safety-focused methods degrade general capabilities. It responds to existing dual-objective approaches that attempt to mitigate this trade-off but rely heavily on large general-purpose datasets or auxiliary reward models. SafeSteer connects to prior work on activation steering for model control and draws on knowledge distillation techniques, particularly on-policy distillation frameworks. The core insight that safety features are sparse in the output distribution extends understanding from work on localized model modifications and targeted fine-tuning, suggesting that global trade-offs are unnecessary when safety violations occur in limited regions of the output space.
enables: SafeSteer opens directions for more efficient safety alignment methods that require fewer resources and less general-purpose data. The localized distillation approach could enable further refinement of safety token selection algorithms, potentially moving toward fully automatic identification of safety-critical regions. Future work might extend this framework to multi-objective alignment beyond safety, applying the principle of localized modifications to other values or capabilities. The method could facilitate rapid safety updates without catastrophic forgetting, enabling safer deployment and faster iteration on alignment techniques. This approach also suggests potential applications to other alignment problems where modifications should be confined to specific output distributions or behavioral domains.
